summary: I currently work as the director of Tourism at Mount Sterling, Kentucky, and I've been here for almost 16 years since January 2009. I moved back here when I was 13, and now I live on High Street in Mount Sterling. The community here is wonderful, and I love organizing events and promoting tourism to make Mount Sterling a fun and energetic place to visit.

I serve as the director of Tourism for the Montgomery County Tourism Commission, which includes Mount Sterling. I grew up around this area after moving back from Ohio at age 13, and I graduated from Bath County High School nearby. My work focuses on promoting tourism throughout Montgomery County and making the community a better place for residents and visitors alike.

I was actually born in Middletown, Ohio, because my parents moved there for work after my dad returned from the army. My dad worked at Armco Steel in Middletown, which was a big employer for people from Eastern Kentucky like my family. I lived there until I was 13 before moving back to Kentucky, and I have fond memories of growing up there surrounded by music and community.

My mother is from Bath County, specifically Preston, and after moving back from Ohio, I attended Bath County High School and graduated there. I was involved in the Radio Club at Bath County High, which gave me early experience in radio broadcasting. Bath County is a big part of my roots, and it shaped my passion for music and community involvement.

Preston, Kentucky, is where my mother is from, and it’s part of Bath County where I spent my high school years. My family has strong ties there, and after moving back from Ohio, I lived nearby and attended Bath County High School. Preston represents part of my heritage and the community that welcomed me back as a teenager.

My father was from Menifee County, Kentucky, and although he passed away, his roots in Menifee are an important part of my family history. When my parents met after my dad returned from the army, they eventually moved to Ohio for work but always wanted to come back home to Kentucky. Menifee County is part of where I come from and where my family’s story began.

Armco Steel was where my dad worked after returning from the army, and it was the reason our family moved to Middletown, Ohio. Many people from Eastern Kentucky, including my dad and his friends, found employment there. Armco Steel represents the connection between Eastern Kentucky and Ohio for families seeking work in the steel industry during that time.

I graduated from Morehead State University in December 1991 with a degree in Radio, Television, and Public Relations Advertising. My time at Morehead helped prepare me for a career in media and marketing, which I pursued after graduation. It was a key step in my journey before moving into the cable and radio industries.
cues: degree in Radio, Television, Public Relations Advertising

summary: After returning from Dallas, I worked in Lexington selling TV commercials for what is now Spectrum, though it was called TCI back then. I stayed in Lexington for about six years, working in television advertising and then transitioning into radio, which is my passion. Later, I also sold real estate in Lexington before moving back to Mount Sterling to be closer to family.

When I first started selling TV commercials after college, the cable company was called TCI, which is now known as Spectrum. I sold advertising for popular channels like ESPN and CNN during my time there. This experience gave me valuable insight into media sales and marketing early in my career.

In the summer of 1992, I moved to Dallas, Texas, to work in the TV market after getting an offer from my company. Although I was Rookie of the Year there, I quickly realized I was homesick for Kentucky’s natural beauty. After just about a year, I returned to Kentucky, encouraged by my dad, who helped me find a position in Lexington so I could stay with the company but be closer to home.

I worked in radio sales and on-air roles for what is now called iHeartRadio, though during my tenure the company changed ownership several times. This was during my six years working in radio in Lexington, where I developed my passion for the medium. Radio has always been a key part of my career and personal interests.

WMST is the local radio station in Mount Sterling where I started working after moving back home. I walked in asking for a job to pay my mortgage, and they welcomed me immediately. I’ve been there for almost 17 years, doing morning shows, weather, commercials, and news, which keeps me connected to the community and supports my tourism work.

Owingsville has its own radio station, but when I was in high school at Bath County, we would ride up to WMST in Mount Sterling to do a radio show every Saturday morning. This connection helped me build my early experience in radio broadcasting. Owingsville is part of the regional community that shares media and cultural ties with Mount Sterling.

Paul Mullins is a really famous bluegrass musician whom I grew up around. My dad often had bluegrass musicians come to our house to play music, and Paul Mullins was part of that musical community. His presence influenced my lifelong love of music and bluegrass traditions.

Tom T. Hall was a name we often talked about growing up because he was a well-known musician connected to the bluegrass and country scene. While I’m not sure if he ever visited our house, he was definitely part of the musical environment I was raised in. His music and legacy were familiar to me from an early age.

JoJo's Jam Session was a very popular radio show when I was about 15 or 16 years old, and it played soft rap music that we couldn’t hear anywhere else. It was a big deal for my friends and me, and we were glued to the radio to listen to it. This show was part of my early radio listening experience and helped spark my interest in broadcasting.
